statement.markdown: Write a function to validate the exposed ports of a Docker container. Given a Docker container object with an `exposed_ports` property, validate that each exposed port is a valid port number (between 1 and 65535). If a port is not valid, return an error message. If all ports are valid, return the container object with the validated ports.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be a Docker container object with an `exposed_ports` property, which is an object with port numbers as keys and container names as values.
statement.outputFormat: Output will be the container object with the validated ports, or an error message if a port is not valid.
statement.notes: Port numbers are in the range 1-65535. Port 0 is not a valid port number.

constraints.description: Port number must be between 1 and 65535.

examples.explanation: All ports are valid, so the container object is returned with the validated ports.
Port 0 is not valid, so an error message is returned.
Port 65536 is not valid, so an error message is returned.

hints.text: Check the range of valid port numbers.

editorial.title: Docker Exposed Port Mapping Validator
editorial.intuition: Validate each port number in the `exposed_ports` object to ensure it is between 1 and 65535.
editorial.bruteForce: Loop through each port number in the `exposed_ports` object and check if it is within the valid range.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a single line of code to validate all ports at once.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use the `Object.keys()` method to get an array of port numbers, then use `every()` to check if all ports are valid.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: If all ports are valid, return the container object with the validated ports. If any port is not valid, return an error message.
commonMistakes: Not checking the valid range of port numbers
